steps.title: Understand what would make the concern unjustified
steps.content: The concern was that virtual tours would make in-person visits seem unnecessary. For the administrators' claim to hold, a survey result must show the opposite: that virtual tours did not suppress — or better yet, actively encouraged — in-person attendance. The right answer will show that viewing a virtual tour made people more likely to visit in person.

steps.title: Eliminate three choices, confirm one
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Many surveyed visitors to the Galleria dell'Accademia indicated that the virtual tours convinced them to plan an in-person visit.': Virtual tours directly caused people to plan a visit — the exact opposite of the fear. This directly refutes the concern. ✓
  • Choice B — 'Most surveyed visitors to the Galleria dell'Accademia indicated that they were unaware of the virtual tours before their first in-person visit.': If visitors didn't know about virtual tours, those tours couldn't have influenced their decision either way — this doesn't support the claim. ✗
  • Choice C — 'Most surveyed visitors to the Galleria dell'Accademia indicated that they lived somewhere other than Florence.': Geographic origin says nothing about whether virtual tours affect the choice to visit. ✗
  • Choice D — 'Many surveyed visitors to the Galleria dell'Accademia indicated that they would likely view the virtual tours in order to reminisce about their in-person visit.': Using tours after a visit tells us nothing about whether tours deterred or encouraged the visit in the first place. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
